title: Chapter 36: The Last Painting
content: The lighthouse was cold.

Not the living cold of the curse, not the reaching cold that pressed against your skin with intention. Just cold — the ordinary kind that fills a stone building when no one tends the fire and the sea wind finds every crack. I stood in the doorway of Julian's studio and felt it settle against my forearms, my neck, the backs of my hands. Just temperature. Just physics.
That was the strangest part.

I had expected something. A whisper, a pull, the magnetic drag I'd felt every other time I'd climbed these stairs — that terrible certainty that the paintings needed me, wanted me, were hungry for me. But the studio was just a room. The paintings were just canvas stretched over wooden frames, covered in oil and pigment, propped against walls or hung from hooks. I could smell turpentine. I could smell linseed oil. I could smell the faint chemical bite of cadmium yellow and the mineral dust of ultramarine blue.
Just paint. Just work.

I had come here to burn them. That had been the plan — walk in, commandeer whatever accelerant I could find, and reduce six portraits and one unfinished canvas to ash and smoke. It seemed obvious. It seemed like justice. The paintings had been weapons, hadn't they? The mechanism by which the Council consumed Buena women, generation after generation. Why wouldn't I destroy them?

But I stood there in the cold studio, and I looked at Maren's portrait propped in the corner where Julian had left it, and I understood something with the slow, clear certainty of dawn breaking: these weren't his paintings.

He had made them, yes. His hand, his brush, his technique. But the subject of a portrait does not belong to the painter. The image of a woman is not the painter's property simply because he captured it. That was the Council's logic — that capturing something was the same as owning it, that rendering a woman in oil gave you dominion over her life, her narrative, her self. It was the logic of the curse itself, dressed in aesthetic clothing.
I would not burn my ancestresses.
I would take them home.
I started with Maren.

Her portrait showed her at the kitchen window — the one in the house on Perimeter Road, the one I'd known my whole life without knowing it was hers. In the painting, ivy grew across her mouth, thick and verdant, as if the garden had claimed her voice. Julian had painted it lovingly, that ivy. Every leaf was perfect. Every tendril curled with deliberate grace.

I set the portrait on the easel, steadied my hands, and reached for Identity Recovery — not to break the painting but to break the lie within it. The magic felt different now. Without the curse's weight behind it, without the Council's architecture, it was just concentration. Just intent. I pressed my palm flat against the canvas and felt the paint respond, the surface growing almost warm under my touch, as if the oil remembered it was supposed to be malleable.
The ivy on Maren's lips began to change.

It didn't crumble or burn. It receded — pulled back like a tide going out, like a vine uncurling — and where it had been, words appeared. Not painted words. Pressed words, as if someone had laid a journal page against wet paint and lifted it away, leaving only the ink behind. I recognized the phrasing even before I finished reading it. Maren's journal. The passages I'd found in the walls, the ones about listening to the house speak. The ivy had been covering her words. Now they were on her lips where they always should have been.
*I heard the foundation shift and I knew it was the town settling, not the house dying. Everything settles. Everything finds its level.*
I exhaled. The paint smelled like turpentine and nothing else. No bitter curse-resin. No scent of consumption.

Cordelia was next. In her portrait, she stood at a desk with a ledger open before her, and the ledger was written in her husband's hand — Thomas Ashworth's cramped, possessive script, the same script that had appeared on deeds and contracts, the same script that documented women as property. I pressed my palm to the canvas and watched the handwriting change. Letter by letter, the cramped script loosened, opened, became something fuller and more confident. Cordelia's handwriting. I'd seen it on the marginalia of those same ledgers, the corrections she'd made in the corners, the sums she'd recalculated when Thomas wasn't looking. Now her hand filled the page. The numbers didn't change — she'd always been right about the numbers — but the authority of them shifted. They were hers.
Iris's portrait made my throat tighten.

She sat in a chair with her arms empty, and the emptiness was the whole point of the composition. Julian had painted loss itself — a woman defined by what she didn't hold. I pressed my palm against the canvas and waited.

Light bloomed in her arms. A child appeared, rendered in pale gold, translucent and luminous. I held my breath. Then the child changed — it softened, its edges blurred, and it reformed as a book. Not a baby she'd lost. Her story. The one she'd been told she couldn't write, the one the Council said a grieving woman had no right to. Iris held her book the way a mother holds a child — close, against her chest, with the careful tenderness of someone who knows what it costs to create something the world doesn't want you to create.

Hazel. My grandmother. The portrait showed her standing in a field — the field behind the house, the one she'd tended for decades — and the signature at the bottom read *E. Thornwood*. Not her name. Never her name. The Council had given her a married name and a married life and expected gratitude.

I laid my hand on her canvas and the signature shifted. The letters rearranged themselves with calm precision, like a typesetter correcting a galley proof. *E. Thornwood* became *HAZEL BUENA*. In full. No initials. No hiding. She'd signed her initials in the wood of the house because she couldn't sign her name anywhere else. Now her name was on her own portrait.

Vivienne's was the last of the completed ones. She stood at a window — always at a window — looking out. Waiting. The composition was familiar; I'd seen it in a hundred paintings of women. The woman at the window, gazing toward something outside the frame. Waiting for a ship. Waiting for a man. Waiting for permission to leave.

I pressed my hand to the canvas. The window remained. But Vivienne turned away from it. She walked toward the viewer, toward the edge of the frame, and her face — which had been in profile, softened by the light of what she was watching — now faced forward. Sharp. Decided. She wasn't waiting for anything.

The sound the paint made as it shifted was faint — a whisper of friction, like cloth against cloth. I hadn't heard it before because the curse had been too loud. Now, in the quiet of a disarmed studio, I could hear the painting correcting itself. Each brushstroke settling into its proper place. It sounded like a sigh of relief.
Then there was the seventh.
The unfinished portrait. Mine.

It sat on Julian's largest easel, the one by the window where the northern light fell. The canvas showed a woman's body from the shoulders up, and the body was rendered with obsessive care — the collarbones, the slope of the neck, the fall of dark hair. But the face was blank. A pale oval of primed canvas, waiting.

Julian had been saving my face. He'd been waiting to paint it last, after the curse had done its softening work, after I'd been properly consumed. He'd wanted me docile before he gave me a likeness. He'd wanted my eyes to be gentle. He'd wanted my mouth to turn up at the corners, soft and pliable and grateful.
I stared at the empty face for a long time.

The cold was settling into my fingers now. I could feel it in my joints, that particular stiffness that comes from standing still in a room that the sea wind has claimed. My breath made small clouds. The easel creaked — a wooden sound, an ordinary sound, nothing supernatural about it.

I could destroy it. I could take the canvas off the stretcher bars and rip it in half and walk away. That would be the story everyone expects, wouldn't it? The heroine burns the villain's lair. The captive smashes the mirror. We know how that narrative goes.
But I didn't want to destroy his painting.
I wanted to paint over it.

I crossed to Julian's supply table. His brushes were still there, laid out in a row the way he'd kept them — organized by size, bristles pointed uniformly upward. His palette was crusted with dried paint, but there were tubes of fresh pigment beside it. Titanium white. Ivory black. Yellow ochre. Burnt umber. Raw sienna. The colors of a face. The colors of *my* face.

I picked up a brush — a small round, the one that would give me the finest control. The handle was warm from my grip, or maybe from the sunlight that had been falling on the table. I squeezed pigment onto a clean section of palette. I mixed. My hands knew what to do. I'd been mixing paint since I was old enough to hold a brush, since before I understood that the house was listening and the fog was watching and my name carried a curse. This was the first work I'd ever done. The first language I'd ever spoken.
I turned back to the easel.
The blank face waited. An empty theater. A question without an answer.
I lifted the brush and painted my own eyes.

Not soft. Not grateful. Tired — genuinely tired, the way I actually was, after months of fighting and losing and fighting again. The dark circles beneath them were real. The crow's feet at the corners were real. I painted them faithfully. I didn't smooth them away or soften their severity. I was thirty-two years old and I had earned every line.

I painted my nose — the bend in it where I'd broken it falling from the orchard wall at fifteen, the slight asymmetry that made my face interesting instead of pretty. Julian would have straightened it. Julian would have given me a nose that fit the classical proportions, a nose that didn't carry a story he didn't write.
I painted my mouth last.

This was the part he would have gotten most wrong. He would have painted my lips slightly parted, yielding, on the verge of speech that was really on the verge of agreement. A mouth designed to receive rather than to declare. I painted my mouth closed. Firm. The lower lip slightly fuller than the upper — accurate, that was just genetics — but set. A mouth that had said *no* and meant it. A mouth that had said *this stops with me* and meant it. A mouth that had told the truth when lying would have been easier, and a mouth that had learned, finally, that silence in the face of theft is not the same as consent.

Each stroke of the brush made a sound. Not a loud sound. A small, decisive *swish* against canvas — the sound of a decision being made visible. The sound of paint becoming evidence. Stroke after stroke, the sound accumulated into something that felt, impossibly, like a conversation. Not with Julian. He was gone. With myself. With the version of me who had walked into this lighthouse weeks ago, terrified and determined and not yet sure if determination was enough. I was answering her now. I was showing her what we looked like when we chose.
I set the brush down.

The face on the canvas stared back at me, and it was not beautiful in the way the other portraits were beautiful. It was not composed. It was not consumable. It was me — sharp-edged, exhausted, unmistakably real. The face of a woman who had walked into the machine and walked out the other side carrying things the machine had tried to destroy.
I had painted myself as I was.
I picked up the brush one more time, this time for the signature.
In the lower right corner, where Julian would have signed his name — where he had signed his name, on every other portrait, small and precise and proprietary — I wrote two letters.

Like Hazel. Like every Buena woman who'd had to hide her full name but left her initials as evidence. I was carrying the same tradition, the same coded resistance, the same quiet insistence on existing in the record even when the record was designed to exclude me.
Then, smaller, beneath those initials, I added:
*The seventh. Who chose.*

He had painted us without our consent. I painted myself with full knowledge of what the act meant. That was the difference. That was always the difference. It wasn't the brush or the pigment or the composition. It was the *choosing*. The difference between a portrait and a mirror is agency. He took our images. I took them back.
I carried the paintings out of the lighthouse one by one.

Maren first, then Cordelia, then Iris, then Hazel, then Vivienne, then my own. Seven portraits under my arms and against my chest, the stretched canvas pressing lines into my forearms as I navigated the narrow spiral stairs. Each step was a physical negotiation — the paintings were large, the stairwell was tight, and I had to turn sideways twice to keep the frames from catching on the stone walls.
But I got them all down. Every single one.
I stepped out of the lighthouse door and into the morning.

There was no fog. That was what I noticed first — not the dramatic, cinematic parting of mist, not a supernatural clearing. There was simply no fog. The air was ordinary morning air: salt and ocean and the green-growing smell of the vale coming back to itself. Sunlight fell without obstruction. The sea was visible all the way to the horizon, where it met the sky in a line so clean it looked like it had been drawn with a ruler.

I set the paintings down on the grass, leaning them against the lighthouse wall in a row. Seven portraits. Six corrected, one newly made. The wind touched them — just wind, nothing sentient — and the canvases shivered, and I stood there looking at them and felt something settle in my chest like a foundation finding its level.

I had wanted to burn them. I'd been ready to. And maybe that would have been simpler — the dramatic gesture, the cleansing fire, the satisfying crumble of ash. But simple isn't the same as right. Burning the paintings would have destroyed the evidence. It would have erased what Julian did, made it invisible, given the same silence the Council had always given us. I'd had enough of silence. I'd had enough of things disappearing.

These paintings — corrected, reclaimed, signed with our own hands — they were evidence now. Not the supernatural kind. Not the cursed kind. The ordinary, durable kind. The kind that says: this happened. This woman lived. This man tried to overwrite her, and she wrote herself back.
I pulled my journal from my coat pocket. The pages were soft with use, and the morning breeze tried to take a few of them. I held them flat and wrote:

*He painted us as he wanted us to be. I painted myself as I am. The difference between a portrait and a mirror is agency. He took our images. I took them back. That's not revenge. That's restoration.*

I looked at the row of paintings one more time: Maren speaking, Cordelia writing, Iris holding her story, Hazel signing her name, Vivienne walking away. And me — sharp and tired and unmistakably present, my own hand on my own image, my own name in my own initials.
Seven women. Six corrected. One who chose.
I picked the paintings back up and started the walk home.
